entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Music Appreciation, Hymns & Spirituals, Folk Songs, Sol-fa: Musical Games, Afternoon Listening

About Music Appreciation
This course aims to foster a love of music. Students begin by learning about and listening to common orchestral instruments. Then they spend the rest of the course with a few famous pieces of music that serve as a fun introduction for students with no prior exposure to classical music.

About Hymns & Spirituals
Students sing hymns and spirituals to build their faith and appreciation of God's work across time. Hymns and spirituals are connected to the historical time period as appropriate.

About Folk Songs
Students sing folk songs connected to the time period (1800-1900).

About Sol-fa: Musical Games
Students play musical games to notice rhythm and melody.

Hymns & Spirituals
The Christian hymn and spiritual tradition is incredibly rich, and it is vanishing from many churches. In addition to bringing a much-needed moment of peace to your day, hymns can also introduce students to important theological ideas and church liturgy.

Folk Songs
The purpose of learning folk songs is twofold: to increase students’ enjoyment of music and to situate folk music in its historical context in an effort to give students a richer understanding of the times and places that birthed the songs. A musical score is sometimes included for songs, but does not always exactly match the audio used, since folk songs are primarily oral songs and often take many different forms. Studying folk music helps students grow deep roots in our shared heritage and gives insight into historical events from the perspective of the people who were there.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Music Appreciation
☐ Term 1: Download the audio files or bookmark the web page of A Child’s Introduction to the Orchestra. 
☐ Term 2: Learn how to pronounce Prokofiev and Camille Saint-Saens. 
☐ Term 3: Art and music are paired together throughout this term. If teachers anticipate the resources might be overstimulating, Wild Symphony by Dan Brown is a good alternative (see Extra Helpings).

Hymns & Spirituals
☐ Bookmark the Alveary hymns, spirituals, and folk song playlist for the year, or create your own YouTube, Spotify, or Amazon playlist if desired.

Sol-fa: Musical Games
☐ Bookmark the Musical Games Playlist (in Quick Links), which includes many selections from the term.

blocks.text:
• Connect each lesson to the previous one for that topic. 

• For music appreciation study, a recap might look like a brief discussion about the name or sound of the piece or instrument you listened to previously. 

• For hymns and folk songs, this might be an effort to hum the melody or recite the words of the song you're currently learning.

Prepare to listen to the day's musical selection or activity by inviting students to consider an interesting musical or historical idea in the lesson, perhaps a unique word, rhythm, instrument, or piece of historical background (usually included in lesson plans).

• Listen to the day's musical selection or practice the activity. 

• Music Appreciation lessons will usually have you listen to a piece of music for 3-8 minutes. If students have a difficult time listening for the duration of the lesson, stop the music at their limit.

• Movement is always a great way to encourage students to engage with the music. Sometimes the lessons will provide a suggestion, but students may make up their own using their whole bodies or creating finger plays. They may also draw, color, or act out a dance with stuffed animals, dolls, or other figures.

• Hymn, folk song, or musical games will sometimes have you listen to a part of the song and then practice echoing back the melody and/or words.
Let students share any thoughts or connections they have about the lesson.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Moving and Music

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
“Here We Go Round the Mulberry Bush.” Follow the movement directions, ensuring that larger groups or students with spatial perception differences have enough room.
∞ Video Link: Here We Go Round the Mulberry Bush

➜ SING
Now try singing the song and make up some new actions (possibilities: “this is the way we clap our hands,” “sweep the floor,” or “dance around”).

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage

PREP: Bookmark #57 in Our Hymns, Our Heritage.

➜ INTRO
Our first hymn this year is called "How Firm a Foundation" 

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Today, focus on getting familiar with the hymn. Read the words aloud and listen to the full song. Note that the recording includes a 4th verse which we are not learning this term (feel free to learn it as well if you're interested!). There are also a few other renditions of this hymn in the playlist if you prefer a different musical style. Just note the fact that the words might be slightly different than the ones in your hymnal/booklet.
∞ Audio Link: How Firm a Foundation YouTube

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Learn the history of the song from your hymnal.
"Text and Tune" p.136
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
Listen to Sibelius' tune which was used as the base for our hymn.
∞ Video Link: Finlandia

• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Use your preferred recording to listen to the hymn in the afternoons, evenings, or anytime! The more you spend time with it, the more the words will sink into your head and your heart.

• IMPORTANT DATE
Be Still My Soul composed by Katharina A. von Schlegel (Germany, 1700s)

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

PREP: Lyrics are included in your Our Work booklet or Music PDF. Print off the tune if you like as well.
∞ PDF Link: Yankee Doodle Sheet Music

➜ INTRO
What does the word "Yankee" mean? Our first folk song of the year is one of the most famous American folk songs, but it actually started as a British insult! Learn more about the history and then listen to the song today.
∞ Article Link: Yankee Doodle History

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Feel free to choose your own version of Yankee Doodle to learn. Just know that the lyrics vary widely, so another audio or video is unlikely to match the words in your Our Work or Music books. 

• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Be sure to listen to the term's songs throughout the week!

• IMPORTANT DATE
Yankee Doodle was first composed (~1755 AD)

Join-in Rhymes

PREP: Print out and read over instructions.
∞ Link: "The Owl" and "Here We Go"

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Read “The Owl” and “Here We Go” aloud and tell the students what they are going to say with you (the italicized parts). You may want to read the poem aloud first and then have the students join in the second time. Tell the students to copy you closely. Exaggerate louds, softs, ascending, descending tones of your voice.

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book
⍞ Materials: World Atlas or Globe

➜ RECAP
What is the name of the hymn for this term and what is it about? What do you remember about how the song came to be written?

➜ MAPWORK
Use your world atlas or globe and locate Germany, Finland, Scotland, and Wales

➜ READ & SING
First read the lyrics aloud in your hymnal or Music PDF. Then practice the tune by first listening to a verse, then humming the next two.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Full Song

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, United States Atlas or a Globe
 
➜ RECAP 
What do you remember about the history of "Yankee Doodle"?

➜ SING 
Listen to the full song, paying attention to the lyrics. Then listen again and hum along. You might want to march as well! Spend some time reading and memorizing the lyrics (use your Our Work or Music booklet).  
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle

➜ EXPLORE
With the remaining time or in the afternoon, explore pieces that have Yankee Doodle incorporated into them! Just as books make references to earlier works, composers often make musical references to other songs. The more you know about music, the more musical references you will catch! It's like picking up on an inside joke in a conversation!
∞ Article & Video: 200 Years of Yankee Doodle

lessons.title: 10m Music Appreciation - Lesson 2

➜ LISTEN, VIEW & NARRATE

Today's songs need your help. In the first one, you need to pretend to be a teapot, and for the second one, you're going to make a lot of animal noises.
∞ Audio Link: I'm a Little Teapot

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book
 
➜ RECAP
Sing the tune of How Firm a Foundation on "hoo" and read the words aloud. Pay attention to the meaning and punctuation as you read.

➜ SING 
Today, listen to a line of the song, then pause the video and echo it back, working your way line by line through the song. This will help you focus on each line of the song, not just the ones which come easily to you.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Full Song
• What images or lines in the song do you like best? 

➜ COPYWORK (Grade 4+)
Copy a line of the melody into your music journal and (if you are taking sol-fa), see if you can label the notes with the sol-fa syllables. The song is in the key of Eb which means that the G the song begins on is "mi."

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the tune of "Yankee Doodle"? 

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Write down the first line of each verse to help yourself remember the order. Then sing along with the recording. If you need to focus more closely on the words, pause the video after a line and echo it back yourself.
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle

➜ WRITE (optional)
Like most folk songs (and even hymns), many different verses have come and gone from Yankee Doodle. If you want a challenge, try composing some of your own additional or alternate verses! Try singing your lyrics with the tune to make sure you have the right rhythm (you'll notice that there are four beats in each line).
lessons.teacherNotes:
• MUSIC CONNECTION
If you play an instrument, try playing the melody of Yankee Doodle!

➜ RECAP
Sing as much of “I'm a Little Teapot” or “Old MacDonald Had a Farm” as you can without the music.

➜ SING
Sing along with the songs today. Make up verses or add any you made up last time.

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)

➜ RECAP
 Recite the hymn (as much as able)

➜ SING
 Try to sing the whole song with the accompaniment. Note the areas where you aren't as sure of the tune or the words.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Tune

➜ READ & DISCUSS
What story in the Bible does the end of the second verse remind you of? Read the story in Mark. 
∞ Bible Link: Mark 4:31-35

• Why do you think Katharina included this reference in the song? How does this story bring comfort?
• What other Bible verses or stories come to mind as you read this hymn?

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing as much of "Yankee Doodle" as you can from memory. Note the spots where you get stuck!

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Read the lyrics and work on any spots you got stuck. Then try singing the song with the instrumental (use your lyrics, not the video's). When it ends, keep singing the rest of the verses a cappella. Make up your own accompaniment by clapping or marching if you like!
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle Tune

➜ DISCUSS
Can you imagine being a soldier and first hearing this tune as an insult? Can you think of any other examples of something that started as a put-down but was then reclaimed to mean something else?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS (Grade 10+)
Read Percival Everett's short story "The Appropriation of Cultures" (linked in Extra Helpings) to think more about how songs or other cultural symbols gain and change connotations.

➜ LISTEN, VIEW & NARRATE

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Dwarfs and Giants

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Listen to "In the Hall..." by Edvard Grieg. Tiptoe with hands on hips when the music is soft. When it is loud, pretend to be a giant and march with your hands high in the air. During the second song, stand in one place, but move your body up and down as the music gets louder or softer. You can also swing your arms and twist with the music. Ensure that larger groups or students with spatial perception differences have enough room.
∞ Audio Link: In the Hall of the Mountain King
∞ Audio Link: Morning Mood
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
 
➜ RECAP 
Sing hymn from memory (as able).

➜ SING 
Now that you know the words better, experiment with how you sing the song. Try singing it faster, slower, softly, loudly, or any other way you'd like to try. Think about how to emphasize the meaning of the words by the way you sing different verses. Are there other instruments you would use? Use the recording as needed.
∞ Audio Link: How Firm a Foundation Tune

➜ READ & DISCUSS
In the original German, there were six verses to the song and then five when it was translated into English, though only three or four of those verses are commonly sung today. Read the original five verses. What do you think of the missing two verses? (Start learning them if interested!)
∞ Article Link: Original Lyrics, "Text: Translation"

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing as much of Yankee Doodle as you can from memory.

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Work on any spots you struggled with in the recap. Then, using the instrumental track or just your voice, sing all of Yankee Doodle (use lyrics if needed). Experiment with how you sing each verse. Try singing one loud and proud, another like you're telling a secret to a friend, etc. If you wrote any of your own verses, add them in as well!
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle Tune

➜ WRITE (optional)
Refine or add more of your own verses to the song!

lessons.body:
Skipping

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Today we're going to have fun skipping! (Teacher should demonstrate how to skip to make sure students know how; if skipping is too challenging, teachers can model a shuffle instead.) Listen to “Skip to My Lou” and practice skipping with the music.
∞ Audio Link: Skip to My Lou
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Children of the Heavenly Father Sing
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage

➜ RECAP 
Warm up your voice by singing "How Firm a Foundation."

➜ INTRO
Time to start a new song! Have you heard of Charles Wesley before? He wrote a lot of different hymns including our new one: Children of the Heavenly Father Sing. What is this song about (if you've heard it before), or what do you think it will be about based on the title?

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Bookmark #74 in Our Hymns, Our Heritage and read the English words aloud. Then listen to the full song (note that there are a few small changes in the video lyrics ("a" Virgin versus "the" Virgin, etc.)).
∞ Video Link: Children of the Heavenly Father Sing Full Song

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Learn the history of the song from your hymnal.
"Text and Tune" p.170

• What did you think of George Whitefield's edits to the first first? Try singing Charles Wesley's first line instead and see what you think!
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
For more on Charles Wesley and his hymns, see hymns #2, 31, 37, 41, 63, 86, and 103.

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, Globe or World Atlas

➜ RECAP
Hum or sing the first few verses of Yankee Doodle.

➜ INTRO
Today we're starting a new folk song about cherry blossoms! The tune originated sometime during the Edo Period (1603-1868) in Japan, which was a time of relative stability and peace. Over the years, it has become internationally famous and has been performed around the world.

➜ VIEW & MAPWORK
Cherry blossoms have been important to Japanese culture for centuries, and festivals to view them in peak season take place every spring. Learn a bit more about this tradition and look at the pictures of some of the beautiful places in Japan. You may want to pull out Google Maps or an atlas and locate the different places mentioned as well!
∞ Article Link: Sakura in Japan

➜ LISTEN 
Listen to the song once or twice and follow along with the lyrics. Print off the sheet music tune if desired.
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura
∞ Image Link: Sakura Sheet Music

lessons.title: 10m Music Appreciation - Lesson 6

➜ LISTEN, VIEW & NARRATE

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Skipping Part 2

➜ RECAP
Skip in a circle. If skipping is too challenging, students may shuffle instead.

➜ PRACTICE
We're going to skip more today, but this time, see if you can skip sideways! Try sideways skipping (left and right). (If there is a group of students, have them take hands and skip sideways in a ring.) Try to make up another way to skip (maybe backwards!). After you feel confident, skip and sing with the music.
∞ Audio Link: Skip to My Lou

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
⬚ 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 7
Children of the Heavenly Father Sing (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF
 
➜ RECAP 
What is Children of the Heavenly Father Sing about?

➜ READ, SING, & DISCUSS
First read the lyrics aloud. Write down the beginning of each verse to help you remember the order. Then work on the tune by listening to the first verse and then humming along with the last two. Slow down and pause the tune after each line and hum the melody back if you run into any tricky spots.
∞ Video Link: Children of the Heavenly Father Full Song

• Look back at the lyrics. How many different descriptions or titles for Jesus do you see? For instance, the first line starts with "newborn King." 

➜ LISTEN
As your hymnal mentioned, Children of the Heavenly Father Sing is sung the world over. With any extra time, look up the hymn in another language you're familiar with or interested in. A version in British Sign Language is linked below to get you started.
∞ Video Link: Children of the Heavenly Father (BSL)
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATE
Hark! the Herald Angels is composed with Whitefield's tweaks (1754AD).

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the beginning of the tune of Sakura, Sakura? 

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Listen to the song and pay attention to how each word is pronounced. Then listen again and pause the video after each line to echo it back. 
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura

➜ VIEW
The song is often performed on the koto, a Japanese stringed instrument which became very popular in the 1700s. Listen to this instrumental version of our song with extra time.
∞ Video Link: Sakura Sakura (Koto)

lessons.title: 10m Music Appreciation - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Percussion and Saxophone

➜ INTRO
Percussion instruments are normally played by striking (some kind of beater [e.g., a mallet] or pedal-operated stick) or shaking. Can you think of any instruments that fit this description?

➜ LISTEN, VIEW & NARRATE
∞ Audio Link: Peter Percussion
∞ Audio Link: Max the Saxophone
∞ Video Link: George Meets the Orchestra (6:01)
∞ Image Link: Saxophone

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Guessing Instruments

PREP: Think of how you want to imitate different instruments for your student to guess.

➜ PRACTICE
Imitate various instruments and have your students guess what they are. If students find this excessively difficult, do it together or make it a funny game of charades.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
⬚ 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 8
Children of the Heavenly Father Sing (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you recite or sing the first verse without looking at the lyrics?

➜ SING 
Practice singing Children of the Heavenly Father Sing today. Pause the video after each line and sing it back. This will help you learn the song in its entirety and focus on each line. 
∞ Video Links: Children of the Heavenly Father Full Song

➜ DISCUSS
As our hymnal mentions, many different biblical references are woven into this hymn. Look back at the list on p.26 of Our Hymns, Our Heritage and pick a few verses to look up. Do you see the parallels? Are there other stories or verses the song reminds you of?

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP

lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP

➜ LISTEN & SING 
Listen to the full song once, following along with the words. Then try to sing the song yourself a cappella (with just your voice).
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura

➜ STUDY
If you take sol-fa or an instrument, look at the sheet music of this folk song and count how many different tones are used. The same note in a different octave doesn't add to the count (for instance, just count one B). How many different tones did you find? [Answer: 5] Much of Japanese music is composed with a scale of five notes, and this song uses the hirajoshi scale (see examples in the link below). This scale is a default scale for the koto instrument as standardized by a famous Koto player in the 17th century, Yatsuhashi Kengyō. Play around with these five notes and see what other tunes you can come up with, or try playing Sakura starting on a different note than shown in the sheet music, but using the same scale.
∞ Image Link: Sakura Sheet Music
∞ Article Link: Hirajoshi Scale

➜ READ & LISTEN 
We're starting a new song! Bookmark #118 in Our Hymns, Our Heritage. We don't know exactly when O Worship the King came into existence, but it is a spiritual, and as slavery grew in the American colonies during the 18th and 19th centuries, Christians who were enslaved started singing songs reminding themselves of God's power and the hope he brings beyond our sufferings. 
∞ Video Link: O Worship the King (note that the recording says "toils" instead of "trials" on verse 2. Either is fine to sing.)

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
En roulant ma boule
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, colored pencils, or markers

➜ RECAP
Sing Amazing Grace from memory (or look at lyrics if needed). Think about the words and try to sing in a way that matches them!

➜ INTRO
We're learning a new folk song today! In the 17th and 18th centuries, France claimed a significant portion of land in North America (see map link below). France's financial situation eventually collapsed, and they lost the Seven Years' War with England in 1763, but French voyageurs spent decades paddling through the Great Lakes and down the Mississippi and Missouri rivers (see Extra Helpings for more on the Voyageurs!) to transport furs. As they paddled, they sang to pass the time and keep their strokes even. Our folk song for the rest of this term was one of the songs the voyageurs sang.
∞ Map Link: New France in its Heyday

➜ STUDY
The story of this song is odd and looks long, but you'll notice why it's a good paddling song when you listen to the tune and rhythm. But first, look at the lyrics in your Our Work/Music book. With your colored pencils or markers, mark, highlight, or underline the lines which are repeated (for instance, use the same color to highlight/mark the second and fourth line of each verse, which are ALWAYS the same).

➜ LISTEN
If you can, follow along with the lyrics in your book as you listen. Pay attention especially to the refrain today and focus just on learning those words.

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
En roulant ma boule
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Tell what you remember about the people who often sang "En roulant ma boule" in the 1600s and 1700s.

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
As French voyageurs paddled, they often sang in a call-and-response format where the leader sang one line and the rest of the men responded. Try singing "En roulant ma boule" in this style by singing along on repeated sections. For instance, let the singer sing the first two lines of the refrain, then you join in when it's repeated. Similarly, listen to the first two lines of each verse and then try joining in when they are repeated. (The singer tries to naturally mimic this call-and-response style by singing louder on the repeated sections.)
∞ Video Link: En roulant ma boule

Hallelujah! What a Savior
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF

➜ INTRO 
Do you remember Charles Wesley who wrote Children of the Heavenly Father Sing We're learning another of his hymns this year!

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE 
Read the words aloud from your Our Work/Music PDF. Note that verse 5 is not included in our video recording, so mark that out or bracket it off to learn on your own time if interested! Charles Wesley wrote this at his conversion and the original song had 18 verses! Discuss any words that are new to you, then listen to the full song.

Our Hymn, Our Heritage "Text & Tune" p.202

What do you remember about "En roulant ma boule" from last term? We're learning two more songs that might have been sung on the water this term! The first is called "Shenandoah."

➜ READ & SING 
Staying true to folk tradition, the lyrics for Shenandoah have multiple historical roots and are a bit cryptic. Read the words in your Our Work/Music book, then listen to the song and read the video caption.
∞ Video Link: Shenandoah

➜ INTRO
We'll be listening to a variety of works this term. Look at the artwork, then read the questions related to each piece. Listen to each piece several times. Some students may not need to have the artwork and questions available during listening, but others might. Allow them to engage in whatever way is best for them. Can you hear the different features in the music?

➜ LISTEN, VIEW & NARRATE
∞ Audio Link: Flight of the Bumblebee
∞ Art Print Link: Chrysanthemums Hiroshige

• Can you hear the bees buzzing above the flowers? 
• What instruments do you hear? Which ones describe the bee?

These lessons are inspired by the Metropolitan Museum of Art's book "Can You Hear It?" which is linked in Extra Helpings but is currently out of print. If you are able to obtain the book, you can use it with these lessons as it puts the art and music selections in one place and provides information about instruments.

lessons.body:
⬚ 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 30
Hallelujah! What a Savior?
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing "Hallelujah! What a Savior."

➜ INTRO
Our last hymn for the year is from the Appalachian region of the US and has some beautiful themes related to Easter and Jesus's death and resurrection.

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Read the lyrics aloud and then listen to the full song (choose one of the two options). 
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V1
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V2 (note that one line in verse 4 is slightly different, so adjust in your book if using)

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Learn the history of the song from your hymnal.
"Text & Tune" p.178

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Michael, Row the Boat Ashore
⍞ Materials: Like a Bird, Our Work or Music PDF, atlas

PREP: If this book is new to you, read p.5 with your students. 

➜ RECAP 
Sing Shenandoah to warm up your voice and refresh your memory.

➜ INTRO
Unlike our previous folk songs from the north and Midwest, "Michael Row the Boat Ashore" comes from the east. Read a little about the song from Like a Bird, p.13.

➜ LISTEN
Read the lyrics in your Our Work or Music book and then listen to the full song.
∞ Video Link: Michael, Row the Boat Ashore

➜ MAPWORK
Look up the Georgia Sea Islands in your atlas or online.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Spelling and Clapping

➜ SING
We're going to start learning a spelling-and-clapping song today. With each verse, the singer is going to drop a letter of BINGO, the dog’s name in the song. You need to clap or stomp your feet instead of singing the letter. By the end of the song, you'll be doing a lot of stomping and clapping. Listen to the song first, so you get an idea of how it goes.
∞ Video Link: BINGO with Lyrics

Hallelujah! What a Savior? (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF and US Atlas

➜ RECAP
Tell what you know about "Hallelujah! What a Savior?" Do you know where Appalachia is on the map? Locate it!

➜ SING 
Hum a favorite tune to warm up your voice. Then alternate between listening to a verse and "hooing" a verse to practice the tune.
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V1
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V2

➜ DISCUSS
Did you notice how many phrases are repeated in the song? How do you think the song would be different if it didn't have so much repetition?

Hallelujah! What a Savior? (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the tune of Hallelujah! What a Savior?

➜ SING
If you are feeling confident with the melody, sing the full song along with the recording. For any sections that you find difficult, pause your preferred recording after a line and echo it back. 
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V1
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V2

➜ EXPLORE
Listen to the ways a few different musicians have adapated and performed this song. What are the different instruments you notice? How do different performers make the tune their own?
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V3
∞ Video Link: Hallelujah! What a Savior V4

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
Michael, Row the Boat Ashore
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the whole tune of "Michael, Row the Boat Ashore?"

➜ SING 
Take the role of the "leader" today and sing the first part of each line (not the "Hallelujah").
∞ Video Link: Michael, Row the Boat Ashore

➜ STUDY
No one knows exactly when this song first started, but possibly as early as the 1700s, rowers would make up their own verses. 
∞ Song Link: Additional Lyrics

• How do these verses add to your understanding of the song? 
• Consider learning a few more verses OR making up a few of your own.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
In a group context, teachers can divide the class and have one half sing each line while the others respond with the "Hallelujah."

• Can you hear "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star" on the piano? The book Can You Hear It says, "This is one of the composer's jokes - 'Twinkle, Twinkle' is an old tune that will never die, and so it, too, is something of a skeleton!"

➜ SING 
Sing "Hallelujah! What a Savior?" on "hoo." Then sing the song with the accompaniment (be sure to use the words in your PDF or hymnal) and check your work. Go back and listen to the full song if needed.

terms.content:
Hymns & Spirituals
· Sing "Be Still My Soul" OR "Hark! The Herald Angels Sing."

Folk Songs
· Sing "Yankee Doodle" (add in your own verses if you came up with some!) OR "Sakura, Sakura" and tell a little about the song.

Sol-fa: Musical Games
· Sing a favorite song, do a favorite activity/dance, or draw/paint a favorite piece.

Hymns & Spirituals
· Sing "Just a Closer Walk with Thee."

Folk Songs
· Sing "Amazing Grace" and the refrain (or more if you want) of "En roulant ma boule."

Sol-fa: Musical Games
· Sing a favorite song, do a favorite activity/dance, or draw/paint a favorite piece.

Hymns & Spirituals
· Sing "What Wondrous Love is This" or "O for a Thousand Tongues to Sing."

Folk Songs
· Sing "Shenandoah" and tell what you remember about Chief John Skenandoah.

Sol-fa: Musical Games
· Sing a favorite song, do a favorite activity/dance, or draw/paint a favorite piece.
